mechanism: Primary pharmacology is sympathomimetic: ephedrine/pseudoephedrine increase adrenergic signaling (direct and indirect effects), raising heart rate, blood pressure, and CNS stimulation. This mechanism is established for the alkaloids, while whole-herb effect size varies by alkaloid content and co-ingredients (e.g., caffeine).

nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference:
contraindications: avoid with uncontrolled hypertension, arrhythmias, or stimulant medications
safetyNotes: NCCIH and FDA emphasize serious safety risks with ephedrine alkaloids, including cardiovascular and neurologic adverse events. Avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding and in people with cardiovascular disease, hypertension, arrhythmia, or seizure risk. Self-directed use for weight loss/performance is not considered safe.

sources.title: FDA: Final rule on dietary supplements containing ephedrine alkaloids

description: Ephedra sinica is a traditional medicinal shrub whose stems were used for respiratory and stimulant purposes. Modern safety concerns dominate current risk-benefit assessment for ephedrine-alkaloid-containing supplements.
dosage: No safe over-the-counter dietary-supplement dosing recommendation is provided for ephedrine-alkaloid ephedra products in the U.S. due to regulatory prohibition and safety concerns.

interactions: Additive stimulant/cardiovascular risk with caffeine and other sympathomimetics.
Potential opposition/instability with antihypertensives and other cardiovascular medicines.
Avoid combination with MAO-inhibiting or other activating agents due to blood-pressure/CNS risk.
preparation: Historically used as dried stem preparations (decoctions/extracts). U.S. ephedrine-alkaloid dietary supplements were removed from lawful marketing following the 2004 FDA final rule.
region: Native to parts of Asia and cultivated/harvested for traditional use; commercial products historically varied in alkaloid content.
therapeuticUses: traditional support for alertness and fatigue reduction
summary: Ephedra sinica (ma huang) contains sympathomimetic ephedrine alkaloids. In the U.S., dietary supplements containing ephedrine alkaloids are banned because FDA determined they present an unreasonable risk of illness/injury.
